item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Expect a rewrite of this sometime soon. This is not the level of quality I hold myself to on the wiki.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not sure what I think about a site Director so blatantly ignoring the dangers of utilizing a SCP of this one’s caliber (being incredibly unpredictable and dangerous)

But overall a well written piece with effectively stark images

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My line of thinking was- for the longest time, the object was only focused on Civil War dioramas, and teleported from one to another in an unbroken chain. Once the Foundation was finally able to get the jump on the object, they simply removed it from the diorama and shoved it in a cell before an Infestation Event was complete. The object needs to complete the event before teleporting. Interrupting it basically would render it inert until the object is exposed to another Civil War diorama. This was something that was in the first draft, alongside a larger description of the ‘how’ the object worked, but it was removed for, well, being too long and unnecessary. So, unless you have a Civil War diorama, it’s pretty much safe- or at least, it was before it focused its efforts to the Foundation.

I’d like to think when testing the object, the Foundation was extremely careful with the duration of the tests, and set up a few decoy dioramas around Area 179 just in-case it teleported.

I’ll take what you said about the -As and -Bs into consideration. I think that was my “in-universe” specifics side speaking. Changing those up would certainly help the flow of the article.

Regardless, I appreciate the upvote. Thanks for reading!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Some critique
Begone

Maybe I'm misunderstanding something about the anomaly, but how was it Safe or Euclid when it "spontaneously appears in museum diorama scenes relating to the American Civil War"? That sentence screams Keter, but I feel like something was lost between drafts about how it can be contained, as they seem to have it in custody in the addenda. Does it spontaneously manifest or does it have to move itself there? Is there some limit that keeps it from teleporting out of Foundation custody and into a diorama in Kentucky? If there is, I don't believe it's covered in the article.

Why is there a distinction between SCP-5035-A and SCP-5035-B? Neither has any major effect on the story of the article, and it would be simpler to just lump in "the modification of diorama pieces" and "the manifestation of objects" as one singular power. Better yet, I think it'd be a more elegant approach to just label the "infestation events" as SCP-5035-A and be done with it, as it covers everything that SCP-5035 can do.

Last thing. SPaG:

The words 'POWER' are carved into the skin of the drum.

Despite the my gripes, I'm gonna +1 this article anyways, because it's neat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thats understandable, its still a decent article. Good Job!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There isn’t a distinction. Unfortunately the events log got cut short in the drafting process and I decided to keep the most effective events. There were confederate dioramas that didn’t make it into the final article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If 5035 is sending a rather graphic message about reparations and similar themes, why is only going after union exhibits?
The union fought for the emancipation of african americans within the south, and had african american cavalry and infantry units.
Wouldnt it make more sense for it to go after confederate exhibits?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First skip. Thank you to cybersqyd and A Random Day for the greenlights.

Draft critique was graciously provided by JustBixby and cybersqyd.

And the biggest thanks tostormbreath, for coding an entire table for me! I can't thank you enough for that one.

Let's see how this does.

Image Credit
